title: Vaccinium arenicola
intro: Vaccinium arenicola, also known as Sand Blueberry, is a small, shrub-like plant that belongs to the heath family (Ericaceae) and is native to the coastal regions of the southeastern United States. The health benefits of Vaccinium arenicola include its potential to help manage blood sugar levels, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antioxidant properties due to its high content of anthocyanins and other polyphenolic compounds. The bioactive constituents of this plant include anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. Vaccinium arenicola is often used to make herbal teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as being incorporated into various supplements and dietary products.
taxonomy: Vaccinium arenicola is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Ericaceae, commonly known as the heath family. It belongs to the genus Vaccinium, which includes other types of blueberries and cranberries. The species Vaccinium arenicola is native to the southeastern United States, specifically in sandy coastal areas. As a member of the Ericaceae family, Vaccinium arenicola shares characteristics with other heath family plants, such as having small, white flowers and producing edible fruits. The specific classification of Vaccinium arenicola includes its full botanical name, genus, and family.

side_effects: Vaccinium arenicola, commonly known as sand blueberry or coastal blueberry, is a plant native to the Pacific coastal regions of North America. Like other Vaccinium species, Vaccinium arenicola is likely to be toxic if ingested due to its high concentration of glycosides and anthocyanins. Ingestion of Vaccinium arenicola may cause gastrointestinal irritation, nausea, and vomiting. The plant's toxicity can also cause skin irritation or allergic reactions in some individuals. However, the severity and specific side effects of Vaccinium arenicola exposure may vary depending on the individual and the amount consumed.
